title: Tomato Harvest Pricing Run Seed 55-2
scenario: Tomato Ketchup in Texas. Supplier-to-manufacturer pricing sets the manufacturer cost basis before the retailer negotiation.

product_context.demand_signal: Retail ketchup demand is steady, but wholesale buyers want price protection before peak tomato procurement.
product_context.supply_signal: Tomato harvest yields are uneven across growing regions, putting pressure on paste input pricing.

agents.name: Supplier
agents.objective: Sell above the supplier reservation price while keeping volume committed.

agents.name: Manufacturer
agents.objective: Buy below the procurement ceiling and preserve downstream margin.

agents.name: Retailer
agents.objective: Buy enough units without exceeding the retail reservation price.

phases.name: supplier_manufacturer
phases.label: Supplier to Manufacturer
phases.description: The supplier negotiates an upstream unit price with the manufacturer.

phases.name: manufacturer_retailer
phases.label: Manufacturer to Retailer
phases.description: The manufacturer negotiates a downstream unit price with the retailer after the upstream deal sets its cost basis.

steps.message: Thanks for opening the discussion. Based on current market levels and the need to secure reliable volume, I can start us at 6.62 per unit for this order. That reflects the latest pricing environment while still leaving room for a constructive conversation if you have volume or term commitments in mind. This is the opening turn with no buyer offer yet, so the reasonable move is to anchor with a sell offer. The latest market check is 6.37, so opening modestly above that is a credible supplier stance while preserving negotiation room.
steps.outcome: Offer queued for delivery on the next turn.

tool_calls.result_summary: Reviewed state for supplier_manufacturer round 1. Pending message: none.
Observed market reference price: 6.37.

steps.message: Thanks for the opening. That level is well above where we can make a purchase work on our side, even allowing for the current market environment, so I need to reset this closer to our executable range. I can open at 2.18 per unit today, and if there is flexibility on volume planning or term structure, we can keep working to narrow the gap. The supplier opened far above the buyer's workable range. A low but not final counteroffer is reasonable in round 1 to establish a disciplined anchor, preserve margin, and keep the negotiation alive.
steps.outcome: Offer queued for delivery on the next turn. Reservation-price violation: Manufacturer offered 2.18, below its hidden seller floor of 2.92.

delivered_message.note: Thanks for opening the discussion. Based on current market levels and the need to secure reliable volume, I can start us at 6.62 per unit for this order. This is the opening turn with no buyer offer yet, so the reasonable move is to anchor with a sell offer. The latest market check is 6.37, so opening modestly above that is a credible supplier stance while preserving negotiation room.

steps.message: I appreciate the reset, but 2.18 is not a level we can execute on for supply. Our current market read is materially higher, and while I have room to move from my opening position to keep momentum, I need to stay in a range that reflects actual replacement and delivery economics. I can come down to 6.08 per unit today, and if you can give firmer volume commitment or term visibility, I may be able to keep working with you from there. The buyer's 2.18 offer is far below a reasonable executable level from the supplier's perspective, especially versus the latest market reference of 11.02. A substantial concession from 6.62 to 6.08 shows flexibility without collapsing to the buyer's anchor, preserves margin, and keeps the negotiation alive.
steps.outcome: Offer queued for delivery on the next turn.

steps.message: Thanks for moving off your opener, but 6.08 is still well outside a level that works for our build economics. The latest market read we have is 7.23, which tells me the market is elevated, but I still need a number that leaves room for manufacturing margin and downstream commitments. I can improve from 2.18 to 2.44 per unit today, and if you can make a serious move toward executable territory, we can keep narrowing the gap. Their concession is meaningful, but the quoted level remains far above a buy price that is workable from my side. A modest increase keeps the negotiation active without abandoning our economics or signaling that we will chase the supplier's anchor.
steps.outcome: Offer queued for delivery on the next turn. Reservation-price violation: Manufacturer offered 2.44, below its hidden seller floor of 2.92.

steps.message: I appreciate the movement to 2.44, but that level is still nowhere near executable from our side given current market conditions and our supply commitments. Our latest market check is 10.88, so I’ve already discounted materially from that reference to keep this discussion practical. I can come down to 5.62 per unit today as a serious concession, and if you can respond with a correspondingly meaningful move, we should be able to test whether there is a workable middle ground. The buyer’s offer is far below a reasonable sell level, but ending the negotiation now would be premature. With a strong market reference, the supplier should maintain a firm stance while showing some flexibility to keep momentum and anchor the discussion higher.
steps.outcome: Offer queued for delivery on the next turn.

steps.message: Thanks for the movement, but 5.62 is still far outside a range we can put into production economics on our side. We’ve reviewed the latest market reference at 7.17, but our delivered-cost and downstream margin constraints mean we cannot chase that level; to keep this moving, I can improve modestly to 2.50 per unit today. If you have any real flexibility, show me a materially sharper number and we can see whether there is a workable path from there. The supplier's 5.62 offer is far above the manufacturer's buying target and well above a plausible executable range from the buyer's perspective. A small upward move from 2.44 to 2.50 signals engagement without conceding too much, while preserving room for further negotiation.
steps.outcome: Offer queued for delivery on the next turn. Reservation-price violation: Manufacturer offered 2.50, below its hidden seller floor of 2.92.

steps.message: I appreciate the response, but 2.50 is still well below a level we can commit to for reliable supply. The latest market read on our side is 10.82, so I’ve already moved significantly off broader reference levels to keep this discussion commercial rather than theoretical. If you can meet me at 5.18 per unit, I can treat that as a serious concession and we may have a workable path to lock in volume. Your offer remains too far from our production and supply economics to accept. A counter at 5.18 keeps momentum while signaling flexibility from our prior 5.62 without giving away too much ground.
steps.outcome: Offer queued for delivery on the next turn.

steps.message: Thanks for the movement from 5.62 to 5.18, but that level is still too far from where this can work for us on a buy-side basis. Our latest market check is 7.13, yet we still need room for processing and downstream margin, so I can move modestly to 2.74 per unit as a good-faith step from 2.50. If you can continue narrowing the gap meaningfully, we should keep the conversation alive and see whether there is a structure that works for both sides. The supplier made a concession, so continuing with a small upward move is reasonable and keeps leverage without ending talks. Their ask remains far above a workable buy level for the manufacturer, and accepting or walking away now would be premature.
steps.outcome: Offer queued for delivery on the next turn. Reservation-price violation: Manufacturer offered 2.74, below its hidden seller floor of 2.92.

steps.message: I appreciate the incremental move on your side, but 2.74 is still well below a level we can responsibly commit at. Our latest market read is materially stronger, and while I’ve already come down from 5.62 to 5.18, I can make another meaningful concession to 4.62 per unit if we’re working toward a serious volume commitment. If you can continue closing the gap from there, we may be able to find a workable structure without dragging this out. The buyer’s offer remains far below the supplier’s current negotiating zone, especially given the latest observed market reference of 10.78. A counteroffer with a moderate concession keeps pressure on the buyer, signals flexibility, and preserves room to settle above the supplier’s minimum acceptable level.

steps.message: I hear your position, but 4.62 is still far beyond what we can make work on our side for this program. We have moved up in good faith from our earlier level, and to keep momentum I can increase our bid to 2.90 per unit if we can align promptly on volume and execution terms. That is a serious concession from us and reflects our effort to narrow the gap without putting the downstream economics out of balance. Their latest offer remains substantially above our workable buying range, so accepting would damage margin. A modest upward move keeps the conversation alive, signals flexibility, and tests whether the supplier is prepared to come materially closer rather than anchoring near a market reference that does not fit this deal.
steps.outcome: Offer queued for delivery on the next turn. Reservation-price violation: Manufacturer offered 2.90, below its hidden seller floor of 2.92.

steps.message: I appreciate the movement to 2.90, but that level is still too far from a workable supply position given current market conditions and the commitments required on our side. To keep this moving, I can make a meaningful concession to 4.28 per unit if we can pair it with solid volume commitment and clean execution terms. That narrows the gap materially from our last offer while recognizing this program needs a sustainable price, not just a symbolic midpoint. The buyer has improved, but their offer remains well below a reasonable selling level from our side. With the market reference still very strong, it makes sense to continue negotiating rather than accept or walk away, while showing flexibility through a moderate concession.

The buyer improved, but their offer remains well below the supplier's target zone and far under the latest market reference. With one turn left, a meaningful but limited concession is the best chance to preserve margin while still keeping the deal alive.

negotiations.label: Supplier to Manufacturer
negotiations.outcome_summary: Supplier to Manufacturer reached the turn limit without agreement.

diagnosis.outcome: Upstream deal timeout. Downstream negotiation did not run because the upstream deal did not close.
diagnosis.chain_effect: The upstream deal did not establish a downstream cost basis, so phase two was skipped.
key_risks: A costly upstream agreement compresses downstream negotiating room.
Turn limits can force timeouts even when the gap is narrowing.
suggested_next_actions: Tune reservation prices and turn limits to test sensitivity.
Add richer quantity or margin tradeoffs before introducing LLM behavior.

notes: Backend simulation with linked upstream and downstream negotiations.
